title: Big-O of Nested Loops
prompt: A function iterates over every pair (i, j) of elements in an array of length n, doing O(1) work per pair. What is its time complexity?

title: Process vs Thread
prompt: Which statement about processes vs. threads is most accurate?

options.text: Threads in one process share the same virtual address space.
Processes share heap memory by default.
Threads have separate PID entries in the kernel.
Context-switching between threads is always slower than between processes.

title: Index Selection
prompt: A Postgres table 'orders' has 50M rows. Queries filter on customer_id (~200 distinct) and then order by created_at. Which index is most selective for the lookup?

title: Explain Deadlock
prompt: Describe the four Coffman conditions for deadlock and explain one strategy that prevents at least one of them in a multi-threaded resource scheduler.
rubricBullets: Names all four conditions: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait.
Picks a concrete strategy such as total ordering of locks to break circular wait.
Explains how the strategy integrates with a scheduler rather than a user-space wrapper.
Acknowledges at least one tradeoff (e.g. reduced concurrency, cost of acquiring all locks up front).

title: Describe TCP Handshake
prompt: Walk through the three-way TCP handshake. Then describe one failure mode you have seen in production and how it was diagnosed.
rubricBullets: Correctly describes SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK with sequence and acknowledgement numbers.
Picks a real failure mode (SYN flood, half-open connections, MSS blackhole).
Mentions a concrete diagnostic tool (tcpdump, ss, netstat).
Connects the failure to an observable customer-visible symptom.

title: Detect Graph Cycle
prompt: Given a directed graph as an adjacency list, return true if and only if it contains a cycle.

title: LRU Cache
prompt: Implement an LRU cache with O(1) get and put. Capacity is provided via the constructor.

testCases.input: put a 1; put b 2; get a
put a 1; put b 2; put c 3; get a
put a 1; get a; put b 2; put c 3; get a

title: Database Migration Cutover
prompt: Your team is cutting over a read-heavy service from MySQL to Postgres this weekend. The SRE and product manager are in the room. Walk through the go/no-go conversation you lead 30 minutes before the freeze window.

roleContext.you: Senior backend engineer owning the migration

stakeholders.position: Wants explicit rollback command and a monitoring plan.
Customer success has already posted the maintenance banner; slip means re-posting.

expectedOutputHint: Show explicit go/no-go criteria, a rollback recipe, and the moment the PM is given decision authority.

title: Explaining a Schema Choice
prompt: A junior engineer pushes back on your choice of a normalized 3NF schema for a reporting table. Walk through your response.

stakeholders.position: Argues denormalization would be faster to ship.
Needs ad-hoc querying flexibility in week one.

expectedOutputHint: Acknowledge the junior's point, show the second-order read-amplification risk, and name the condition under which you would denormalize later.

title: Transaction Isolation
prompt: Which isolation level prevents dirty reads but allows non-repeatable reads in most SQL databases?

title: Topological Sort
prompt: Given a DAG as adjacency list, return a valid topological ordering, or an empty array if a cycle is detected.

title: Memory Management
prompt: Compare stack and heap allocation. Describe when each is used, and one class of bug unique to each.
rubricBullets: Correctly describes allocation mechanics of both.
Names stack-overflow or dangling pointer for stack; leak or fragmentation for heap.
Shows awareness of language-level escape analysis or GC.

title: Divide and Conquer Complexity
prompt: A recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) solves to which complexity by the master theorem?

title: Greedy Correctness
prompt: Which problem is NOT solved optimally by a greedy algorithm?

title: Binary Search Invariant
prompt: In classical binary search with 'lo' and 'hi' pointers, what invariant must hold inside the loop when searching for the leftmost occurrence of a value?

title: Two Sum
prompt: Return indices of the two numbers in the input array that add up to 'target'. Exactly one answer exists.

title: Longest Substring Without Repeats
prompt: Given a string, return the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.

title: Coin Change
prompt: Return the fewest coins needed to make 'amount' from the given denominations, or -1 if impossible.

title: Pick DP vs Greedy
prompt: Given a problem where each decision affects future options and you are unsure whether greedy is optimal, describe your decision process for picking between greedy and dynamic programming.
rubricBullets: Proposes a concrete test for optimal substructure.
Describes how to construct a counter-example for greedy.
Mentions DP state-space size as a practicality check.
Names at least one classical borderline case (coin change, scheduling).

title: Time-Boxed Problem-Solving
prompt: You have 45 minutes and a problem you do not immediately see a solution to. Describe how you allocate the time.
rubricBullets: Reserves explicit time for reading and clarifying.
Plans a 'brute force first' step.
Commits to a 15-minute stuck-check and what to do next.
Ends with running tests before submission.

title: Live Problem-Solving Interview
prompt: An interviewer asks you to merge k sorted linked lists. You get stuck 10 minutes in. Walk through how you recover.

stakeholders.position: Wants to see your thought process, not just the answer.

expectedOutputHint: Narrate explicitly, switch to a simpler variant (k=2), then generalize.

title: Pairing With a Struggling Peer
prompt: A teammate is stuck on a DP problem with a 24-hour deadline. Walk through the first 15 minutes of pairing.

stakeholders.position: Has written partial, wrong code and is frustrated.

expectedOutputHint: Do not take the keyboard; ask questions that surface the state definition.

title: Commit Hygiene
prompt: Which of the following is the BEST commit-message practice for a PR that fixes a single bug?

options.text: fix: reject empty email in signup form (closes #1214)
A long essay on why JS is bad

title: Feature Flags
prompt: You need to ship a risky migration gradually. Which is the SAFEST rollout posture?

options.text: Merge behind a feature flag defaulted off, enable for 1% of traffic, observe, scale up.
Wait until 100% ready, then deploy to everyone at once.
Push straight to production on a Friday afternoon.
Merge without tests; we can fix forward.

title: CI Break
prompt: Main branch CI turns red with a flaky integration test. First action?

options.text: Disable the test silently and keep merging.
Quarantine the test, open an issue with logs, announce in the team channel.

title: Parse Env Var
prompt: Write a function readIntEnv(name) that reads an integer environment variable with a default of 0, returning NaN only when the value is set but unparseable.

title: Retry With Backoff
prompt: Implement retry(fn, { attempts, baseMs }) that retries an async fn with exponential backoff up to 'attempts' times.

title: Debug a Mystery P95
prompt: Your service's p95 latency jumped from 140 ms to 620 ms this morning. No deploy went out. Walk through how you would investigate, in order.
rubricBullets: Starts with observability: check dashboards and logs for a temporal correlation.
Checks dependency health (database, cache, upstream) before application code.
Considers infrastructure changes (autoscaling, noisy neighbour).
Mentions creating a hypothesis log or timeline document.

title: Review a Risky PR
prompt: A junior teammate sends you a 1500-line PR that rewrites the auth layer. How do you review it?
rubricBullets: Asks for a design document or pre-work before reviewing code.
Requests the PR be split into smaller reviewable chunks.
Calls out security boundaries explicitly.
Plans a pairing session instead of only async comments.

title: Production Incident
prompt: Payments are failing at 3% rate and climbing. You are IC commander for the incident. Walk through the first 10 minutes.

stakeholders.position: Already investigating logs.
Customers are calling in; needs a message.
Wants an ETA.

expectedOutputHint: Designate scribe, comms channel, and rollback owner; set a 10-minute checkpoint.

title: Code Review Pushback
prompt: An engineer disagrees with your review comments on test coverage. Walk through the conversation.

stakeholders.position: Feels tests slow them down.

expectedOutputHint: Name the specific risk. Offer a concrete minimum. Avoid authority framing.

title: Dependency Bump
prompt: Your tool reports a CVE in a transitive dependency. What is the most appropriate first step?

options.text: Ignore — the CVE is transitive, not direct.
Check if the vulnerable code path is actually reachable, then plan an upgrade window.
Immediately force-push a major version bump to main.

title: Idempotent Upsert
prompt: Write a function upsertUser(userId, patch) that is safe to retry and never produces duplicates.
starterCode: export async function upsertUser(userId: string, patch: Record<string, unknown>): Promise<void> {}

testCases.expected: name updated, no other fields touched

title: CAP Tradeoff
prompt: A read-heavy global service tolerates a few seconds of staleness but cannot tolerate downtime. Which CAP posture is most appropriate?

options.text: CP — choose consistency over availability during partition.
AP — choose availability with eventual consistency.
CA — assume no partitions can ever happen.

title: Queue vs Sync RPC
prompt: A request produces a computation taking 4 minutes and the user does not need an immediate response. Which pattern is MOST appropriate?

options.text: 202 Accepted with a status URL and background job via queue.
Long polling the endpoint every second.

title: Cache Invalidation
prompt: You cache compiled user preferences for 1 hour. Users complain that changing a setting 'sometimes' doesn't take effect. BEST first fix?

options.text: Lower TTL to 1 minute and call it done.
Invalidate the cache key on the write path.

title: Design URL Shortener
prompt: Sketch the system design for a URL shortener expected to serve 1000 redirects/second with custom alias support. Include data model, write path, and read path.
rubricBullets: Picks a keyspace strategy (base62 counter vs hash vs user alias) and justifies it.
Separates write path from read path; read path is cache-first.
Handles custom alias collisions.
Mentions at least one failure mode (hot key, counter overflow, cache miss storm).

title: Tradeoff a Monolith Split
prompt: The monolith's deploys have become painful. Make the case for (or against) splitting one module into its own service. Include what you would measure.
rubricBullets: Identifies specific symptoms: deploy time, blast radius, team autonomy.
Names the cost: network boundaries, data consistency, observability.
Proposes a measurable decision criterion before splitting.
Accepts that the answer may be 'not yet'.

title: Rate Limiter
prompt: Implement a token-bucket rate limiter with refillPerSec and capacity. allow() returns true if a request is allowed now.

title: Sharding Router
prompt: Write shardFor(userId, shardCount) returning a stable shard index that is resilient to shardCount changing by one.

title: Architecture Review
prompt: Present your system-design proposal to a skeptical staff engineer. They push on why you chose Kafka over SQS.

stakeholders.position: Thinks SQS is simpler; wants justification for operational cost.
Cares about team learning curve and on-call.

expectedOutputHint: Name the specific requirement (ordering, replay, fan-out) that forces Kafka. Accept SQS if the requirement doesn't hold.

title: PM Scope Negotiation
prompt: The PM wants to ship a 'simple' new feature but the data model requires a migration that will take 4 weeks. Walk through the conversation.

stakeholders.position: Has committed a public launch date.
Worried about concurrent migrations on the same table.

expectedOutputHint: Offer a minimal V1 that avoids the migration; frame V2 as a measurable investment.

title: Idempotency Design
prompt: Describe how you would make a payment-initiation API idempotent for retries.
rubricBullets: Uses a client-provided idempotency key.
Stores request fingerprints with a TTL.
Returns the original response for duplicate keys.
Handles key-reuse with conflicting payloads.

title: Read Replica Fallback
prompt: A read replica lags 30 seconds. A user just wrote and is viewing the page. BEST approach?

options.text: Read-your-writes: pin to primary for the session window after a write.

title: Status Updates
prompt: Which status update is most useful for a manager skimming on a Friday?

options.text: I did a lot of work this week.
Shipped order-cancel flow; 2 flaky tests remain; blocker: awaiting design on edge case X.

title: Meeting Invitation
prompt: You need a 30-minute decision from three stakeholders. Which invitation is best?

options.text: Subject: 'Decision: go/no-go on migration'; agenda with 3 options and a pre-read link.

title: Escalation
prompt: Your PR has been waiting 6 days for review and blocks a release. BEST escalation?

options.text: Add a clear summary in the team channel: what is blocked, deadline, two paths forward.

title: Write a Decision Doc
prompt: Draft the opening 3 paragraphs of a decision document recommending React Query over a bespoke cache layer. Assume an engineering-manager audience who skims.
rubricBullets: Leads with the recommendation in one sentence.
Names the 2-3 criteria that drove the decision.
Acknowledges the strongest counter-argument and why it loses.
Keeps prose concrete and under 250 words.

title: Upward Update
prompt: Write a Monday upward update covering last week: shipped X, blocked on Y, risks for next week, ask of the team.
rubricBullets: Three sections clearly labelled.
Ask is specific and actionable.
Risk is quantified or time-boxed.
Tone is neutral, not defensive.

title: Changelog Generator
prompt: Write a function that turns a list of commit messages into a markdown changelog grouped by conventional-commit type.

title: Pretty-Print Slack Block
prompt: Given a deploy status object, return a Slack-blocks-kit array that highlights failures.

title: Customer-Facing Outage
prompt: Your team caused a 25-minute outage for a large customer. Walk through the apology email you send the customer's VP of Engineering.

stakeholders.position: Wants to know why it happened and what changes.
Wants the messaging to be truthful but careful.

expectedOutputHint: Own the failure, name the root cause, list 2-3 specific changes with dates.

title: Interviewing a Skeptic
prompt: You pitch a new internal tool to a senior engineer who is skeptical. Walk through the conversation.

stakeholders.position: Thinks it overlaps with existing tool.

expectedOutputHint: Ask what would convince them, not just why they are wrong.

title: Meeting Summary
prompt: Summarise a 45-minute architecture meeting into 5 bullets including owners and dates.
rubricBullets: Five bullets or fewer.
Each bullet has an owner and a due date.
Captures unresolved items explicitly.
No meta commentary.

title: Feedback Framing
prompt: Which is the best framing when giving critical feedback to a peer?

options.text: I noticed the last two tasks slipped. What is getting in the way?
I don't want to nag you, but...

title: Train/Val Split
prompt: Why is a time-based split preferred over a random split for a sales-forecasting model?

options.text: Random splits are faster to compute.
Random splits leak future information into training, overstating accuracy.
Time splits are always better for every model.

title: Regularisation
prompt: Your model achieves 97% training accuracy and 64% validation accuracy. FIRST intervention?

options.text: Add regularisation (L2, dropout) or reduce model capacity.

title: Auth Token
prompt: Which statement about JWT is MOST accurate?

options.text: JWT tokens are encrypted by default.
JWT tokens are signed but not encrypted; payload is base64url-encoded.
JWT is safe to use with the 'none' algorithm if the client is trusted.
JWT cannot be revoked, therefore it cannot be used for auth.

title: Feature Leakage
prompt: A teammate's model performs great in dev but collapses in production. You suspect feature leakage. Describe how you confirm it and fix it.
rubricBullets: Enumerates how the feature could have seen the label at training time.
Proposes a timeline-aware pipeline as the fix.
Checks calibration on held-out recent data.
Recognises the production monitoring gap that let it slip.

title: ETL Design
prompt: Design a nightly ETL that ingests CSVs from 40 vendors, validates schema, and loads into a warehouse. Keep it to 250 words.
rubricBullets: Splits extract, validate, load as idempotent stages.
Stores raw input for replay.
Uses a schema registry or per-vendor contract check.
Names one observability primitive (row count delta alert, null-rate alert).

title: Rolling Mean
prompt: Write rollingMean(series, window) returning the simple moving average over a fixed window size. Pad with NaN.

title: One-Hot Encoder
prompt: Write oneHot(column) that returns a matrix with one column per unique category in insertion order.

title: Model Review With PM
prompt: You have a model that improves a business KPI by 3%. The PM asks whether to ship it. Walk through the conversation.

stakeholders.position: Cares about user impact and A/B risks.
Worries about fairness across segments.

expectedOutputHint: Name segment-level performance, rollout strategy, guardrail metrics.

title: Security Review
prompt: You present an API design to the security team. They ask about rate limiting and secret handling. Walk through your response.

stakeholders.position: Focused on OWASP top 10 and secret rotation.

expectedOutputHint: Cite concrete controls (WAF, per-user token bucket, secrets manager with rotation cadence).

title: Cross-Validation
prompt: For a 10,000-row dataset with class imbalance 1:50, which cross-validation scheme is most appropriate?

title: Explain a Model
prompt: A sales manager asks why a customer was scored 'high risk' by your model. Describe your approach.
rubricBullets: Uses feature attribution (SHAP or permutation importance).
Translates numeric features into business language.
Acknowledges uncertainty; does not over-claim causality.
Offers a next step the sales manager can actually take.

title: Owning an Ambiguous Task
prompt: Your manager says 'our docs are a mess, can you look at it?' You have no specifics. BEST next step?

options.text: Guess at what is wanted and start rewriting.
Propose a 2-week scope covering the top 3 painful docs, confirm with the manager, then execute.

title: Missing a Deadline
prompt: You will miss a 2-week commitment by 3 days. BEST action?

options.text: Stay quiet and hope no one notices.
Raise it at the earliest clear signal, with a specific revised date and the reason.
Blame the PM for unclear scope.
Push the work onto someone else.

title: Unclear Escalation Path
prompt: You notice a security vulnerability in an adjacent team's code. You do not know who owns it. BEST first step?

options.text: Post about it publicly on social media.
Report it via the internal security channel or your manager, with reproducible details.
Fix it yourself without telling anyone.
Ignore it — it's not your code.

title: Pick One Thing
prompt: You have 3 half-finished projects and a new urgent ask. Describe how you pick what to actually complete this week.
rubricBullets: Uses a decision criterion (customer impact, reversibility, blast radius).
Acknowledges opportunity cost of WIP.
Communicates the choice to stakeholders proactively.
Reserves time to actually finish one thing.

title: A Judgment Call
prompt: Describe a decision you made where you had to choose between two good options with partial information. What did you decide, and what happened?
rubricBullets: Names the options specifically.
Makes the decision criterion explicit.
Accepts the downside of the path not taken.
Honest retrospective — not all decisions work out.

title: Task Scheduler
prompt: Given a list of tasks with priorities and due dates, return an order of execution that minimises late tasks.

title: Escalation Policy
prompt: Write shouldEscalate(alert) that returns true when an alert has been open past its SLA or has touched a priority-1 customer.

title: Pushback Against Scope Creep
prompt: Your PM adds a fourth feature to a sprint that already has three. Walk through the conversation.

stakeholders.position: Believes the fourth feature is tiny.
Worried about testing window.

expectedOutputHint: Accept OR decline with a specific reason; offer a tradeoff, not a flat 'no'.

title: Reliability Review
prompt: You are on-call and your service has breached its SLO 3 times this month. Walk through the weekly reliability review.

stakeholders.position: Wants a concrete plan.
Offers shared ownership but needs reciprocal investment.

expectedOutputHint: Show concrete causes per breach, a top-3 investment list, and an owner per item.

title: New Language First Week
prompt: You are given one week to become productive in a new language. Which is the strongest single habit?

options.text: Read the entire language specification end-to-end.
Build two small, real tools end-to-end in the language.

title: Switching Domains
prompt: You move from payments to ML infra. What is the highest-leverage first action?

options.text: Rewrite existing code in the new domain's idioms.
Find the three most-used systems and read their design docs before touching code.
Ignore domain conventions because 'software is software'.
Only attend meetings; avoid writing code.

title: Feedback Loop
prompt: Your tech lead gives you feedback you disagree with. BEST response?

options.text: Ask questions to understand the underlying concern, then decide how to act.
Agree in the moment but ignore it afterwards.

title: Learning Plan
prompt: Draft a 4-week learning plan to become productive in a new codebase of 300k LoC. Include concrete weekly outputs.
rubricBullets: Weekly outputs are measurable (merged PRs, doc contributions, on-call shadowing).
Ramps from read-only in week 1 to owner in week 4.
Explicit feedback checkpoints with a mentor.
Acknowledges tradeoffs against ongoing project work.

title: Acting on Feedback
prompt: Describe a piece of feedback you received in the last year, what you did with it, and what changed.
rubricBullets: Specific feedback, not generic.
Clear action taken, with a timeline.
Observable change in outcome.
Honest about what didn't change.

title: Translate JS to Python
prompt: Given a small JS pure function, write an equivalent Python implementation with identical behaviour.

title: Pick Up New Library
prompt: Using a HTTP client library you've never seen, write a function that fetches a URL with a 2-second timeout and retries once.

title: Onboarding Conversation
prompt: You are two weeks into a new team. Your 1:1 with your manager starts. Walk through the first 15 minutes.

stakeholders.position: Wants to know what's working, what isn't, and how to help.

expectedOutputHint: Come prepared with a specific win, a specific block, and a specific ask.

title: Learning-in-Public
prompt: You present a retro of a new technology you tried that partially worked. Walk through the presentation.

stakeholders.position: Wants an honest assessment.
Is considering the same tech.

expectedOutputHint: Cover what worked, what didn't, what you'd try next — no defensiveness.

title: Unblocking Yourself
prompt: You've been stuck on a subtle bug for 3 hours. BEST next step?

options.text: Keep pushing — giving up is weakness.
Write a short rubber-duck summary, then ask a peer with fresh eyes.
Paste the whole file into Stack Overflow.

title: Agility Self-Assessment
prompt: Rate your learning agility 1-10 and justify with two specific examples from the last 6 months.
rubricBullets: Honest self-rating — not anchored at 10.
Two specific, non-overlapping examples.
Measurable outcomes.
Names a concrete area of weakness.
